narrative:
A chilly start yields a dramatically warmer afternoon with abundant sunshine. TGIF! There will be a few more clouds around this weekend, but it certainly won’t detract from the lovely weather to get outside. For next week, we get a true taste of summer as temperatures continue to surge. Unfortunately, prevailing dry weather will continue for the entirety of this forecast cycle.

The RaysWeather.Com Photo Contest is underway for photos to appear in the 2027 Blue Ridge Calendar. We are open for submissions through April 15. We broke all records with 2026 calendar sales and look to top that with the 2027 calendar — it's our 24th calendar. Come on! Hit us with your best shot!

Get the free RWC Mobile App...: Apple and Android

Subscribe Thursday Extended forecast is only available for RaysWeather.com supporters. Subscribe Forecast Discussion Sunshine dominates the weather today. Higher-elevation temperatures at daybreak are much milder than valleys, and that is pointing us to a much warmer afternoon. Like Thursday, some valley locations will have diurnal temperature ranges (the difference between morning lows and afternoon highs) of around 40-degrees. A weak backdoor front slips south into the area on Saturday and then will dissipate on Sunday. Presently, the risk for a passing light shower with this feature is non-zero, but really not worth mentioning as a sensible weather consideration. That dry, “droughty” theme continues for the upcoming workweek as temperatures soar into record-territory by midweek. With the rising temperatures, afternoon relative humidity crashes will enhance our wildfire potential, given the ongoing drought conditions. Be careful with any outdoor burning, and heed any burning bans. A front might get close enough for shower opportunities toward the following weekend. Detailed forecast discussion and analysis is only available to RaysWeather supporters. Subscribe Log in Scott Stephens Chief Meteorologist PHOTO OF THE DAY CLICK TO ENLARGE Pink skies to start the day. Submitted by Michael N. All Photos Submit Photo Latest Insights March 2026 Recap Swings of Spring As it has a tendency to do, March brought out the hats, gloves, coats, t-shirts, shorts, and flip-flops in the same week. We had 50 degree swings in 24 hours, upper 80s in the Foothills, several inches of snow at the high peaks, rumbling thunderstorms, frosts, and even a weak tornado in Mars Hill! Suffice it to say, the tradition of wild springtime swings was upheld, and then some. The early part of the month, through the 10th, was mostly quiet beyond the ups & downs of temperatures. However, a rather active mid-month period began with a remarkably sharp cold front on the 12th. Beech Mountain recorded a temperature of 71.0 degrees on the 11th just before 3PM, and followed that up with a reading of 21.7 degrees just after noon on the 12th - nearly 50 degrees in under 24 hours! 2 to 3 inches of snow for the favored high elevations accompanied this incredible front, the first of only two notable snow events anywhere in the region during the month. Another heavier event followed on the 16th, with 4 to 6 inches at high elevations and 1 to 3 inches in the valleys. Following this active period, the final two weeks of the month were fairly quiet, with a few of the first pop-up thunderstorms of 2026 taking shape at times. Take Boone for example. If you were to draw a line connecting the top of the high temperature bars in the graph below, you'd probably be able to make a decent roller coaster out of it. The mid-month cold snap stands out, but a remarkable number of days made runs into the 60s and 70s. Local Details The prevailing storyline of the beginning of spring is the warmth and dryness we continue to experience. Many locations across the entire Southeastern U.S. experienced a top 5 (or even top 3) warmest March on record, by average temperature throughout the entire month. In tandem with that, much of the area is experiencing a notable level of drought, due to 5 consecutive months of below normal precipitation. The tables below tell the story well:

These are remarkable numbers for an entire month. It is rare to see these sorts of anomalies on the order of 8 to 12 degrees! Hickory fell just shy of a new record for average high temperature in March, with the record of 70.1F (set in 2012) standing strong by just under a degree. The Asheville Airport set three new daily record high temperatures on the 6th, 10th, and 11th. The warmth of the above table is reflected in the cover image for this article.

Most stations fell shy of 50 to 60 percent of average precipitation for March, and the drought monitor has reflected this lack of soaking rains. Springtime is windy in the mountains, and dry grounds and winds do not mix well as far as wildfire danger. A few fires did manage to break out, but thankfully nothing especially widespread developed in March. Below is our anomalous lack of rain in map form, with SWNC and the Foothills worse off than much of the Western Escarpment vicinity. This dry and warm March may cause some fire issues later on as we await the transition into the summertime thunderstorm season. As of the morning of posting this article, there is severe and extreme drought developing in the Southern Appalachians. These maps are courtesy of NOAA. Apr 09, 2026 WWR? Todd Island Park WWR? Todd Island Park. For more information, visit https://toddnc.org/. Apr 10, 2026 Monday Message for April 6, 2026 The first full week of April features some chilly conditions to start, but warming up by late week and during the weekend of the 11th-12th. Dry weather will be with us all week. Apr 06, 2026 See all Golf-O-Meter 0 Golfballs Only ducks or polar bears. Good day for knitting. Maybe, if you are brave. Worth taking a chance. A good day to be outside. Bosses, you'd better stand at the door. (Or will bosses be first out the door?) CONTACT US Privacy Policy Terms and Conditions Contact Us
